data.title: Meta tracking employee keystrokes on Google, LinkedIn, Wikipedia as part of AI training project: report
data.description:
Google (GOOG), LinkedIn, and Wikipedia are among hundreds of websites and apps where Meta (META) plans to capture employee keystrokes and mouse clicks as part of a project to train its artificial intelligence models, according to internal messages viewed by CNBC.

A new employee tracking tool, dubbed Model Capability Initiative ((MCI)), allows Meta to observe and collect data from staffers’ actions on their work computers, Reuters reported on Tuesday. The list of sites being tracked also includes Microsoft’s GitHub, Salesforce’s (CRM) Slack and Atlassian (TEAM).

Meta properties like Threads and Manus are also on the list, which is still in flux and originally included AI apps like OpenAI’s (OPEN) ChatGPT and Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) Claude, according to the report.

The list of third-party sites and services the MCI tool is tracking was widely circulated internally and discussed on chat boards after a member of the Meta Superintelligence Labs, or MSL, sent a memo intended to assuage concerns about worker surveillance and privacy. CNBC viewed the memo.

The data-gathering project is tied to Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg’s ambitious effort to catch up in generative AI, where the company has lagged OpenAI, Anthropic and Google.

Earlier this month, Meta unveiled its first major AI model since the hiring of Wang. Dubbed Muse Spark, the model marked the debut of the new Muse series developed by MSL, the AI unit that Wang oversees.

Like other tech giants, Meta is pushing hard into AI agents that can perform various office and coding-related tasks that are typically accomplished by white-collar workers. A Meta spokesperson confirmed the project but didn’t provide a comment on the list of sites being tracked, the report said.

“If we’re building agents to help people complete everyday tasks using computers, our models need real examples of how people actually use them — things like mouse movements, clicking buttons, and navigating dropdown menus,” the spokesperson said. “To help, we’re launching an internal tool that will capture these kinds of inputs on certain applications to help us train our models. There are safeguards in place to protect sensitive content, and the data is not used for any other purpose.”

Multiple Meta employees characterized the data-tracking project as “dystopian” in internal messages viewed by CNBC. Others expressed concerns that MCI could widely expose sensitive data, including user passwords, details about new product development, and personal information about workers’ immigration status, health, or family members.

The MSL staffer said in the memo that in order to “teach our models to be able to use computers,” Meta requires a “big and unbiased” data set that reflects how employees work and do tasks on their corporate devices.

“We need to capture on-screen content as the context of what was being manipulated or interacted with,” the memo said. In listing a “few assurances,” the MSL representative noted that the new tool would only be able to view employees’ “screen contents” as they see them, and would “not read in files or attachments.”

“Any incidental personal information in your corporate email that may get captured from the screen, will not be learned by the model, due to the mitigations above,” the memo said.

Meta employees who are still concerned about the data-tracking tool, “can control what shows up on your screen by not doing personal work on your work computer,” the memo said.

data.title: Microsoft looked at buying Cursor before SpaceX deal: report
data.description:
Prior to SpaceX’s (SPACE) announcement this week that it’s obtained the right to acquire Cursor for $60B, Microsoft (MSFT) looked at a potential deal for the AI coding startup, CNBC reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

Microsoft, which is trying to boost the popularity of its artificial intelligence tools to keep pace in the booming market for AI tools, chose not to proceed with a bid, said one of the people. Both sources asked not to be named because the discussions were private.

While Microsoft has gained traction among developers with GitHub Copilot, the AI coding market is currently being dominated by Cursor, along with Anthropic (ANTHRO) and OpenAI (OPENAI).

Venture capital firms had lined up financing for Cursor at a $50B valuation, CNBC reported earlier this month.

SpaceX, controlled by Elon Musk, said in a post on X on Tuesday that it agreed to a deal to buy Cursor for $60B by the end of the year, or it will pay the company $10B.

data.title: Thursday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM Jobless Claims

New unemployment claims are compiled weekly to show the number of individuals who filed for unemployment insurance for the first time.

Claims are expected to back up to 210K toward the 4-week moving average of 209.75K from 207K last week.

8:30 AM Chicago Fed National Activity Index

The Chicago Fed National Activity Index (CFNAI) is a monthly index that tracks overall economic activity and inflationary pressures.

9:45 AM PMI Composite Flash

The flash Composite Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) provides an early estimate of current private sector output by combining information obtained from surveys of around 1,000 manufacturing and service sector companies. 

Unlike their European counterparts, these PMIs are seen edging up in the April flash, with the composite up at 50.5 from 50.3 in the March final.

10:30 AM EIA Natural Gas Report

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on natural gas stocks in underground storage for the U.S. and five regions of the country. 

11:00 AM Kansas City Fed Manufacturing Index

The Kansas City Fed index offers a monthly assessment of change in the region's manufacturing sector.

1:00 PM 5-Yr TIPS Auction 

 The US Treasury Department will hold an auction for $24B of 5-year TIPS. 

4:30 PM Fed Balance Sheet

The Fed's balance sheet is a weekly report presenting a consolidated balance sheet for all 12 Reserve Banks that lists factors supplying reserves into the banking system and factors absorbing reserves from the system.

data.title: Altimmune prices $225M public offering of securities
data.description:
• Altimmune (ALT) priced an oversubscribed public offering of common stock, pre-funded warrants and accompanying common stock warrants to raise approximately $225 million in gross proceeds, according to a statement late on Wednesday.
  • Offering includes 64.25M shares of common stock and pre-funded warrants to purchase an aggregate of up to 10.75M shares of common stock.
  • Each security comes with accompanying common stock warrants at exercise price of $3.00 per share. Leerink Partners and Barclays are acting as joint bookrunning managers.

data.title: QuantumScape reiterates 2026 adjusted EBITDA loss guidance of $250M-$275M while ramping Eagle Line QSE-5 output
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: QuantumScape (QS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “In Q1, we completed the installation of the Eagle Line and commenced start-up operations. We are producing initial volumes of QSE-5 cells,” said (President, CEO & Director Siva Sivaram), adding that the team is “continuously improve all aspects of Eagle Line functionality such as equipment uptime, line throughput, control systems and process stability,” and that “we've been integrating advanced AI models into the Eagle Line, and we have seen substantive progress on cell quality and reliability.”

  • (CEO Sivaram) framed the near-term commercialization path with Volkswagen/PowerCo around the next step: “The next phase is field testing. Sales from the Eagle Line will be put through a demanding set of real-world test conditions, and that customer feedback will be used to learn and iterate.” He also said, “in Q1, we shipped cells to an automotive JDA partner for testing,” and that QuantumScape “successfully completed our technology evaluation with another top 10 global automotive OEM customer,” moving to “joint development activities with the ultimate goal of deploying QS technology in their automotive and other applications.”

  • On the capital-light strategy, (CEO Sivaram) said the QS ecosystem is “the cornerstone of our capital-light business model,” and added, “In Q1, we recorded our first customer billings from our ecosystem.”

  • (Chief Financial Officer Kevin Hettrich) provided the quarter’s key financial markers: “GAAP operating expenses and GAAP net loss in Q1 were $109.2 million and $100.8 million, respectively,” and “Adjusted EBITDA loss was $63.2 million in Q1, in line with expectations.”

Outlook

  • “For full year 2026, we reiterate our adjusted EBITDA loss guidance of between $250 million and $275 million,” said (CFO Hettrich). “For full year 2026, we reiterate our capital guidance of between $40 million and $60 million,” he added.

  • (CEO Sivaram) tied operating execution to a Q2 ramp: “In Q2, we plan to ramp QSE-5 cell production to support customer programs across automotive and other applications.”

  • Compared with Q4 2025, management’s guidance language in Q1 2026 stayed consistent on both adjusted EBITDA loss and CapEx, with (CFO Hettrich) again using “reiterate” on the same ranges.

Financial Results

  • (CFO Hettrich) said “Capital expenditures in the first quarter were $10 million,” and “Customer billings for Q1 were $11 million, representing a mix of customer development activities and ecosystem partner payments.” He emphasized, “Customer billings as a metric represents the total value of all the invoices issued by QS to our customers and partners in the period regardless of accounting treatment,” and “The metric is not a substitute for revenue under U.S. GAAP.”

  • Liquidity remained a key balance-sheet datapoint: “We ended Q1 with $904.7 million in liquidity,” said (CFO Hettrich), adding the company “will remain prudent with our strong balance sheet going forward.”

Q&A

  • Yan Dong, Deutsche Bank: Asked for a directional view on QSE-5 output ramp in Q2; (CEO Sivaram) responded, “Q2, we'll begin ramping, and we'll continue to go up satisfying these demands through the rest of the year.”

  • Yan Dong, Deutsche Bank: Asked what investment and product changes are needed for AI data centers; (CEO Sivaram) said, “Most of the learning from automotive business transfers here,” and (CFO Hettrich) added, “The bulk of the investment goes into developing a platform,” with incremental tailoring, while reiterating, “we reiterated our adjusted EBITDA guidance, our CapEx guidance.”

  • Ben Kallo, Baird: Asked whether new-market expansion follows a license model or uses PowerCo; (CEO Sivaram) said, “we will be looking at both of those,” and added, “PowerCo has 5 gigawatt hours of capacity reserved for markets outside the automotive.”

  • Mark Shooter, William Blair: Asked whether VW needs a cell sized beyond QSE-5 for field testing; (CEO Sivaram) answered, “we are field testing with the QSE-5,” while also saying VW’s unified cell work is ongoing.

  • Ayush Ghose, Goldman Sachs: Asked about billings trajectory; (CFO Hettrich) said, “In fiscal year 2025, we recorded approximately $19.5 million in customer billings,” and “In Q1 '26… we recorded $11 million in the quarter,” reiterating, “our guidance is to increase billings year-over-year 2026 compared to 2025.”

  • Laisha Zaack Carrillo, HSBC: Asked whether new markets change automotive timelines and resourcing; (CEO Sivaram) said, “This is not an either/or. We are looking at both of these opportunities,” and noted resourcing was sized “as part of our annual operating plan.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly positive, with repeated probing on timing, ramp rates, and how new markets affect capital needs and automotive priorities (e.g., Deutsche Bank’s request to “characterize the ramp” and HSBC’s question on whether new markets “change the time frame”).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and execution-focused, repeatedly pointing to near-term operational steps and reiterating guidance (e.g., (CEO Sivaram): “Q2, we'll begin ramping,” and (CFO Hettrich): “we reiterate our adjusted EBITDA loss guidance”).

  • Versus Q4 2025, Q1 2026 sounded more operationally concrete on Eagle Line status (“completed the installation… commenced start-up operations”) and more explicit about near-term sampling/ramp, while maintaining the same capital and adjusted EBITDA framing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 advanced Eagle Line from Q4 2025’s “inauguration” milestone to installed-and-running status, with (CEO Sivaram) saying the company is now “producing initial volumes of QSE-5 cells” and planning a Q2 ramp.

  • The “expand into new high-value markets” theme moved from broad opportunity framing in Q4 2025 to a more defined emphasis on AI data centers and defense-related demand in Q1 2026, alongside named governance additions: (CEO Sivaram) said, “We have added Ross Niebergall to the Board and Dr. Mark Maybury as an adviser.”

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s deeper scrutiny of Volkswagen-related cash inflow presentation and EV-market turbulence to Q1’s emphasis on production ramp cadence, conversion of OEM engagements, and practical implications of non-auto market entry.

Risks and Concerns

  • Execution risk around automated ramp was implied in management’s focus on operational parameters: (CEO Sivaram) highlighted improving “equipment uptime, line throughput, control systems and process stability.”

  • Variability in customer-related cash timing remained a recurring caveat: (CFO Hettrich) said customer billings “may vary from quarter-to-quarter due to fluctuations in activity,” and reiterated it is “not a substitute for revenue under U.S. GAAP.”

  • Portfolio-expansion complexity was addressed through resourcing and strategy framing: (CEO Sivaram) said automotive “still remains an important focus,” while new markets are being added, and (CFO Hettrich) described investment as largely platform-based with incremental tailoring.

Final Takeaway

QuantumScape’s Q1 2026 call centered on operationalizing the Eagle Line—now installed, started up, and producing initial QSE-5 volumes—while management reiterated full-year 2026 adjusted EBITDA loss and CapEx guidance ranges and pointed to a Q2 production ramp to support automotive sampling and additional end markets. Management also highlighted a commercialization cadence with Volkswagen/PowerCo moving toward field testing, continued engagement across multiple top-10 automotive OEMs, and early monetization signals from the ecosystem model through first ecosystem-partner billings, while repeatedly cautioning that billings can be lumpy and are not GAAP revenue.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tesla signals over $25B 2025-2026 CapEx as it targets Optimus production by late July/August and Robotaxi in a dozen states by year-end
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tesla (TSLA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Tesla framed 2026 as an investment-heavy year, with CEO Elon Musk saying, "We're going to be substantially increasing our investments in the future so you should expect to see significant -- a very significant increase in capital expenditures," adding that Tesla is "laying the groundwork for significantly increased manufacturing and production."

  • Musk highlighted new-product ramps while cautioning on pace: "We have just started production of Cybercab, and we'll begin production of our Semi truck soon," while also saying initial output will be slow because "whenever you have a new product with a completely new supply chain... you should expect that initial production of Cybercab and Semi will be very slow, but then ramping up... towards the end of the year and certainly next year."

  • CFO Vaibhav Taneja pointed to demand improvements and production constraints, saying, "we have seen a resurgence in demand in EMEA," with France and Germany showing "over 150% quarter-over-quarter growth in deliveries," and adding, "Our biggest limiter continues to be our battery pack capacity, and we are actively working on resolving that."

  • Taneja reported sequential margin and software adoption changes, saying, "Auto margins, excluding credits, improved sequentially from 17.9% to 19.2%," and that FSD adoption reached "nearly 1.3 million paid customers globally," while "the bulk of the growth came from subscriptions" after Tesla "remove[d] the purchase option in some markets in Q1."

Outlook

  • Musk set expectations for a multi-year investment cycle: "we are in a very big capital investment phase, which is going to start now and would last a couple of years," while Taneja said the company expects "negative free cash flow for the rest of the year" as it places orders for "the research semiconductor fab in Austin and for solar manufacturing equipment."

  • On autonomy expansion, Musk said, "we certainly hope to be -- have unsupervised FSD/Robotaxi operating in, I don't know, a dozen or so states by the end of this year," while cautioning that "Robotaxi revenue would not be super material this year" but "will be material probably in a significant way next year."

  • Tesla did not provide explicit EPS or revenue guidance in the transcript, and the provided analysts-estimates JSON did not include a valid quarter tag format for use under the prompt’s validation rules.

Financial results

  • Taneja said Q1 ended with "the highest Q1 order backlog in over 2 years," and that Tesla has already started increasing volume, citing "the Giga Berlin reaching a record output of over 61,000 units in Q1."

  • Taneja described one-time items affecting profitability, saying auto margin benefited from "warranty true-downs around $230 million and some relief on tariffs," and energy gross margin exceeded "39.5%" due to "onetime benefits" including "more than $250 million" tied to prior-quarter tariff recognitions.

  • On cash generation and spending, Taneja said, "On free cash flow, we ended the quarter with just over $1.4 billion," and added, "our current expectation for 2025 -- 2026 is over $25 billion of CapEx," tied to paying for "6 factories" and expanded AI infrastructure.

Q&A

  • Investor question (say.com) on Optimus timing; CEO Musk said, "Start of production is -- we're assuming is somewhere around the late July, August time frame," while also stating, "I don't know what the production rate of Optimus will be this year. It is impossible to predict these things."

  • Investor question (say.com) on unsupervised FSD availability; CEO Musk answered, "probably in the fourth quarter," and described a staged rollout: "we would release unsupervised gradually to the customer fleet as we feel like a particular geography is confirmed to be safe."

  • Investor question (say.com) on Hardware 3 path; CEO Musk said, "Hardware 3 simply does not have the capability to achieve unsupervised FSD," and described offers including "a discounted trade-in" and the ability "to replace the computer" and "replace the cameras"; Executive Officer Ashok Elluswamy added, "we are going to also release a V14 version for Hardware 3... expected to come end of June."

  • William Stein, Truist: asked who funds/builds/operates the Terafab; CEO Musk said Tesla will build a research fab at Giga Texas, "probably a $3 billion-ish initiative," while "SpaceX is going to take care of... the initial phase of the scaled up Terafab"; Stein follow-up on Intel; Musk said, "Intel is excited to partner with us" and Tesla plans to use "Intel's 14A process."

  • Pierre Ferragu, New Street: asked about FSD adoption mix and churn; CFO Taneja said, "you're thinking about it the right way," adding, "we are actually seeing churn of subscribers also coming down"; Ferragu follow-up on Optimus intelligence and Grok; CEO Musk said, "we think we can put a lot of intelligence locally in the robot," with Grok used for "orchestration" and "a low-latency intelligent voice AI."

  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: asked if Tesla’s view on new vehicle models evolved; CEO Musk said, "Cybercab is compact vehicle," and added, "long term, the only manually driven car will be the new Tesla Roadster" and "we may be able to debut that in a month or so"; Delaney follow-up on batteries; CFO Taneja said, "the limiter is not the cells itself. It's the battery pack capacity"; VP Lars Moravy cited actions including Berlin ramping "in-house 4680 cells" and China "ramping in-house LFP module production."

  • Colin Langan, Wells Fargo: asked what metrics support Robotaxi expansion; Executive Officer Elluswamy said, "We track basically all the metrics that you mentioned" and decide expansion based on interventions plus simulation; Langan follow-up on NHTSA/camera updates; Moravy said, "we did change the cameras some months ago" and Tesla expects "to resolve that" with NHTSA, while Elluswamy added, "if the camera is not able to see things clearly... then the FSD won't be available for those cars."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-skeptical, centered on execution and transparency, including Stein seeking detailed responsibility and economics for Terafab, Levy probing "how long is it going to take to ramp," and Langan pressing on "key safety metrics" and an "NHTSA filing" reference.

  • Management tone was positive in ambition but cautious on timelines and uncertainty, including Musk saying "we certainly hope" for a dozen states, "Robotaxi revenue would not be super material this year," and on Optimus output: "It is impossible to predict these things."

  • Versus Q4 2025, prepared remarks leaned more toward concrete project updates (Cybercab production started; Semi soon; research fab plans) rather than the prior quarter’s broader framing around an "amazing abundance" mission and winding down S/X.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Capital intensity increased in specificity: Q4 2025 CapEx was described as "in excess of $20 billion" (and excluded solar/semiconductor fabs), while Q1 2026 expanded to "over $25 billion of CapEx" for 2025-2026 and included early orders for "the research semiconductor fab in Austin" and "solar manufacturing equipment."

  • Autonomy language tightened around measured expansion: Q4 2025 expected autonomy in "somewhere between a 1/4 and 1/2 of the United States by the end of the year," while Q1 2026 shifted to "a dozen or so states" and reiterated safety constraints: "We don't want to have a single accident or injury with the expansion of Robotaxi."

Risks and concerns

  • Taneja flagged margin and demand sensitivity to macro and policy, saying "Both tariffs and sustained high interest rates continue to add to our automotive cost," and warned, "If interest rates continue to rise, our cost of subvention will continue to impact auto margins."

  • Energy profitability and volume were described as volatile, with Taneja noting storage is "inherently lumpy" and Q1 deployments fell "a 38% sequential decline," while adding that normalized margins may compress due to "increasing competition and tariff impacts" given reliance on China-sourced cells.

  • Execution risk was emphasized in scaling Robotaxi and Optimus, with Musk highlighting validation limits and operational edge cases, including vehicles that "get stuck" or enter "literal infinite looping" in construction scenarios.

Final takeaway

Tesla management described Q1 2026 as the start of a stepped-up investment phase, anchored by over $25 billion of 2025-2026 CapEx expectations, a $3 billion-ish research chip fab plan in Texas, and continued spending on autonomy, Optimus, and new-vehicle ramps. Executives highlighted improving auto margin (with one-time items), higher order backlog, and 1.3 million paid FSD customers, while repeatedly stressing that Robotaxi expansion is gated by rigorous safety validation and that early Optimus and new-product ramps will follow a slow initial S-curve before scaling.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Goosehead outlines 2026 revenue growth of 10%-19% while targeting 86% client retention
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Goosehead Insurance (GSHD) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "I'd like to begin by welcoming John Martin as our new Chief Financial Officer. Succeeding Mark Jones, Jr., who has been promoted to President and COO." (President, CEO & Director Mark Miller)

  • "For the first quarter, we delivered strong and consistent financial results, with revenue growing 23% to $93 million, core revenue growing 15% to $79 million and delivering adjusted EBITDA of $24.4 million." (President, CEO & Director Miller)

  • "For the first time in the United States, clients can shop, quote and buy insurance through a true choice model whether that is fully digital, partially digital or entirely human-driven." (President, CEO & Director Miller)

  • "Today, we're excited to announce that clients can now digitally buy multiple homeowners products in Texas with carriers such as SageSure and Mercury." (President, CEO & Director Miller)

  • "Lily, our AI-powered virtual phone assistant is now fully resolving approximately 19% of all inbound calls without requiring transfer to a live agent." (President, CEO & Director Miller)

  • "We have deployed tools behind the scenes in areas such as intelligent case routing, which has allowed us to reinvest roughly 40 full-time service team members towards more complex and value-added interactions." (President, CEO & Director Miller)

  • "The enterprise sales team, which is fueled by our partnership efforts, continued its rapid growth in the first quarter, generating new business growth of over 70% and contributing approximately 20% of the production of new business commissions and agency fees." (President and COO Mark Jones Jr.)

  • "We repurchased and retired 985,000 of our Class A shares, representing $49.8 million." (President and COO Jones)

Outlook

  • "We are reiterating our guidance for the full year 2026. Total revenues are expected to grow organically between 10% and 19%. Total written premiums are expected to grow organically between 12% and 20%." (President and COO Jones)

  • "Throughout the second half of 2026, we expect improvements in client retention from our strategic initiatives and the improving product market to begin to outpace the impact of slower year-over-year pricing in our book of business." (President and COO Jones)

  • "We expect that to result in faster core revenue growth when combined with continued strong new business generation." (President and COO Jones)

  • "We expect to achieve 86% client retention during the year." (President, CEO & Director Miller)

  • Analyst EPS/revenue estimates were provided, but the format does not match the required fiscal quarter validation rules, so no estimates comparison is included.

Financial results

  • "Total revenues for the quarter were $93.1 million, up 23% over the previous year quarter, with core revenues growing 15% to $79.5 million." (President and COO Jones)

  • "Ancillary revenues, which is largely comprised of contingent commissions, was $11.9 million for the quarter, growing 141% year-over-year." (President and COO Jones)

  • "Total written premiums for the quarter were $1.1 billion, growing 13% over the previous year quarter." (President and COO Jones)

  • "Policies in Force grew 14% for the quarter to 2 million." (President and COO Jones)

  • "Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $24.4 million, growing 57% and delivering an adjusted EBITDA margin of 26%." (President and COO Jones)

  • "During the quarter, we demonstrated strong cash generation with $22.9 million of cash flow from operations" and "we ended the quarter with $26 million of cash and cash equivalents and had total debt outstanding of $324 million." (President and COO Jones)

Q&A

  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies: How should investors think about 2026 Policies in Force acceleration and seasonality? President and COO Jones: "Retention is what's going to aid in PIF acceleration" and "the second and the third quarter typically contribute more than the first and the fourth quarter."

  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies: How does digital convenience affect renewal disintermediation risk? President and COO Jones: "the hurdles in the renewal side are even bigger" and "it would not be very easy to automate a lot of what we do."

  • Brian Meredith, UBS: Are digital agent economics/commissions similar, and what is the carrier pipeline? President and COO Jones: "there has been increased demand to have outsized compensation in that partner and digital agent channel"; President, CEO & Director Miller: "We have 4 auto carriers now already on the platform" and "a couple more to be added, TBD, in the next 2 months."

  • Thomas McJoynt-Griffith, KBW: Is digital agent targeted through partnerships, and how is client choice handled with fewer carriers visible? President and COO Jones: "We're not really trying to drive eyeballs to goosehead.com" and "we also are able to build into the platform safeguards and kickout that basically would say... You should talk to an agent."

  • Charles Lederer, BMO: How is Texas exposure trending and why not raise revenue guidance after strong contingent commissions? President and COO Jones: "for the first quarter, 37% of the premium was in Texas" and "we still don't know if there's going to be a hail or hurricanes or fires, right?"

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative (analysts): questions repeatedly probed digital/AI risks, retention durability, and NPS, including renewal disintermediation, "why is it taking kind of longer for retention to improve," and a "declining" NPS trend.

  • Slightly positive (management): prepared remarks emphasized scale, technology, and efficiency with confident framing such as "we are just getting started" and "we believe the time is right to more aggressively expand our offensive capability with more agents and more geographies." (President and COO Jones; President, CEO & Director Miller)

  • Current vs. previous quarter: Q1 focused more on measured operating impacts ("19% of all inbound calls" resolved by Lily; "reinvest roughly 40 full-time" roles) versus Q4’s heavier emphasis on roadmap framing for "Digital Agent 2.0" and AI positioning.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Leadership and structure shifted in Q1: "John Martin as our new Chief Financial Officer" and "Mark Jones, Jr., who has been promoted to President and COO." (President, CEO & Director Miller)

  • Digital Agent progressed from Texas auto/home go-live messaging in Q4 to an additional Q1 milestone: "clients can now digitally buy multiple homeowners products in Texas" with named carriers. (President, CEO & Director Miller)

  • Guidance language remained consistent quarter-to-quarter: "Total revenues are expected to grow organically between 10% and 19%" and "Total written premiums... between 12% and 20%." (President and COO Jones)

Risks and concerns

  • "the first quarter always includes some kind of true-ups" and contingency revenue uncertainty remained tied to catastrophe risk: "we still don't know if there's going to be a hail or hurricanes or fires." (President and COO Jones)

  • Retention and consumer shopping behavior remained a focal point: "people are just kind of frustrated that pricing got so expensive over the last several years" and "they feel like they need to shop it more frequently." (President and COO Jones)

  • Margin cadence risk was framed around hiring and investment timing: "timing of compensation was a little bit delayed" and "we still have some digital agent investments to make, and we want to be leaning into growth right now." (President and COO Jones)

Final takeaway

Goosehead’s Q1 message centered on scaling a “choice” distribution model—“fully digital, partially digital or entirely human-driven”—while tying near-term execution to specific operating proofs, including Lily “fully resolving approximately 19% of all inbound calls” and service automation that allowed the company to “reinvest roughly 40 full-time” roles. Management reiterated full-year 2026 organic guidance of 10%-19% revenue growth and 12%-20% total written premium growth, kept contingent commission expectations unchanged, and emphasized retention improvement toward an expected 86% for the year alongside continued geographic diversification and expansion of corporate offices and partnership-led enterprise sales.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lam forecasts June quarter EPS $1.65 ± $0.15 as it raises 2026 WFE view to $140B with upside bias
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lam Research (LRCX) Q3 2026

Management View

  • "Lam is off to a solid start in calendar year 2026 with revenues and profitability in the March quarter at the upper end of our guidance ranges and earnings per share exceeding the top end of our guided range." (President, CEO & Director Timothy Archer)

  • "We now expect WFE of $140 billion with a bias to the upside as the industry continues to work through various constraints." (President, CEO & Director Archer)

  • "In 2026, we see Lam's served available market, or SAM, percent of WFE expanding to slightly more than the mid-30s percent level, well on track toward our stated goal of high 30s percent over the next few years." (President, CEO & Director Archer)

  • "We now anticipate that this conversion will be pulled forward with the majority of spending occurring before the end of calendar year 2027." (President, CEO & Director Archer)

  • "Lam's advanced packaging revenue growth is expected to exceed 50% in calendar year 2026." (President, CEO & Director Archer)

  • "March quarter revenue came in at $5.84 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Douglas Bettinger)

  • "Customer Support Business Group generated a record $2.1 billion (sic) [ $2.11 billion ] in revenue in the March quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

Outlook

  • "We're expecting revenue of $6.6 billion, plus or minus $400 million." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) "Gross margin of 50.5%, plus or minus 1 percentage point." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) "We're forecasting operating margins of 36.5% plus or minus 1 percentage point." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) "We're forecasting record earnings per share of $1.65, plus or minus $0.15." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "I would encourage you to kind of keep it roughly in the levels that we just guided you to in June." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "We now expect WFE of $140 billion with a bias to the upside" (President, CEO & Director Archer), compared with the prior quarter call: "Our initial 2026 view is for WFE to be in the $135 billion range." (President, CEO & Director Archer)

Financial Results

  • "March quarter revenue came in at $5.84 billion, which was up 9% sequentially and up 24% from the same period in 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "Gross margin in the March quarter was 49.9%." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) "March quarter operating margin was 35%." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "The March quarter diluted earnings per share came in at a record of $1.47." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "For capital return in the March quarter, we allocated approximately $800 million to share buybacks" (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) and "we also retired $750 million of unsecured notes that reached maturity using cash from the balance sheet." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "Cash and cash equivalents totaled approximately $4.8 billion at the end of the March quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) "Days sales outstanding was 64 days" (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) and "inventory turns improved to 2.9x." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

Q&A

  • Timothy Arcuri, UBS: Asked how gross margin reached the "50.5%" level so quickly and how to think about durability; Executive VP & CFO Bettinger: "keep it roughly in the levels that we just guided you to in June." President, CEO & Director Archer tied margin benefits to reliability: "That also yields benefits for us in terms of installation and warranty spending, which flows through to gross margin."

  • Christopher Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about '27 visibility/slotting and CSBG sustainability; President, CEO & Director Archer: "we're having conversations with customers about '27" and "it feels like it's setting up to be a pretty good year in '27 right now." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) On CSBG, Executive VP & CFO Bettinger: "I think it's going to kind of sustain roughly at these levels as we go through the remaining quarters in the calendar year."

  • Harlan Sur, JPMorgan: Pressed on monetizing productivity improvements and on OpEx trajectory/target updates; President, CEO & Director Archer: "How do we monetize it? Yes, it's through services" and "in some cases, new tool sales." Executive VP & CFO Bettinger: "we need to give you an updated framework, and we will do that later in the year."

  • Atif Malik, Citi: Asked what changed in NAND and why down payments are at a low while WFE is expected to grow; President, CEO & Director Archer attributed NAND change to AI data center demand and conversions: "that's what's caused this acceleration." Executive VP & CFO Bettinger: "the group of customers that generally provide the down payments aren't the ones that are growing the quickest."

  • Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein: Challenged whether NAND conversion implies larger services/upgrades and whether WFE can step up further in 2027; Executive VP & CFO Bettinger: "it's not all just upgrades" and on next year, "clean rooms are going to be more available next year" and "WFE is going to be nicely growing next year." President, CEO & Director Archer added: "etch and deposition intensity rises" as clean rooms target advanced nodes.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, focused on sustainability and constraints, including "how much capacity you have?" (Timothy Arcuri, UBS) and "what's wrong with that logic?" (Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein), with repeated probing on services durability, margins, China exposure, and the magnitude/timing of 2027 growth.

  • Slightly positive management tone, emphasizing durability, multiyear visibility, and execution, including "we remain confident in Lam's setup for continued outperformance." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) During Q&A, management stayed measured on specifics, for example: "we can't comment on any specific customer" (President, CEO & Director Archer) and "we're not going to give you a number" on lead times. (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management language appeared more assertive on the upcycle and forward visibility, moving from "clean room space constraints" (President, CEO & Director Archer, prior call) to "WFE of $140 billion with a bias to the upside" (President, CEO & Director Archer, current call), while analysts continued to test durability of margins and how constraints translate into 2027 upside.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance and demand framing tightened upward: prior call "Our initial 2026 view is for WFE to be in the $135 billion range" (President, CEO & Director Archer) versus current call "We now expect WFE of $140 billion with a bias to the upside" (President, CEO & Director Archer).

  • Prepared remarks emphasis shifted toward specific share/portfolio wins and scaling the installed base opportunity, including: "we achieved both dielectric etch wins at a key foundry/logic manufacturer" (President, CEO & Director Archer) and "we delivered our first $2 billion-plus revenue quarter" in CSBG. (President, CEO & Director Archer)

  • Analyst focus stayed consistent on margins, services, China, and the clean-room constraint, but the current call added sharper questioning on the implied magnitude of NAND conversion/upgrade dollars and how that translates into Lam’s services growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We expect that China revenue in the June quarter will decline from these levels." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) Management linked lower down payments to mix: "down payments tend to come from smaller customers and a lot of them are in the China region." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • Constraint risk remained central: "only so much that can be executed in this year" (President, CEO & Director Archer), with management describing the setup as constrained by "various constraints" even while maintaining an upside bias for WFE.

  • Profitability/opex scrutiny increased as spending rises: "We will be growing R&D investments throughout the remainder of the year" (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) and "we also undertook a small workforce optimization focused on efficiency." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

Final Takeaway

Management described record March-quarter revenue and a higher June outlook, alongside an upward revision in its 2026 WFE view to $140 billion with an upside bias. The call highlighted AI-driven inflections raising deposition and etch intensity, a pulled-forward NAND conversion timeline with most spending before the end of calendar 2027, and advanced packaging revenue growth expected to exceed 50% in calendar 2026. In Q&A, analysts repeatedly tested the durability of gross margins and the sustainability of services growth, while management pointed to operational efficiencies, tool reliability, and monetization via services and new tool sales, and reiterated plans to provide an updated long-term model later in the year.
